title: The Complete Guide to .gitignore
content:
What Is .gitignore?

If you use Git to manage your code, you have probably heard of the .gitignore file. But what exactly is it, and why does every developer need it?

A .gitignore file is a plain text file that tells Git which files and folders to ignore. When you run git add or git commit, Git looks at this file and skips everything listed in it. Those files will not be tracked, staged, or committed.

In simple terms: .gitignore keeps your Git repository clean by excluding files that do not belong there.

Why Does This Matter?

Without .gitignore, you might accidentally commit passwords, API keys, huge build folders, or editor config files.

These can cause security risks, slow down your repository, and clutter your project history.

Every professional project — big or small — uses a .gitignore file.

How Does .gitignore Work?

When Git checks your project folder for changes, it reads the .gitignore file. Any file or folder that matches a pattern in .gitignore is completely skipped by Git. It is as if those files do not exist from Git's perspective.


This check happens every time you run git commands. Git reads the .gitignore rules from top to bottom. Later rules can override earlier ones.


Where Do You Put the .gitignore File?

You can place .gitignore files in several locations. Each one affects a different scope.


  • Root .gitignore — This is the most common. Put it in your project's main folder. It covers the whole project.

  • Subfolder .gitignore — Rules only apply to that folder and its contents.

  • Global .gitignore — Applies to every Git project on your computer. Good for editor files like .DS_Store or Thumbs.db.

  • .git/info/exclude — Local rules that are never shared with others. Only you see them.


How to Create a .gitignore File

Creating a .gitignore file is very simple. Here are several ways to do it:

Method 1 — Create It Manually

Open your terminal and run this command in your project folder:

touch .gitignore

Then open the file in any text editor and add your rules.

Method 2 — Use GitHub's Template When Creating a Repo

When you create a new repository on GitHub, it offers a dropdown to pick a .gitignore template based on your language (Node, Python, Java, etc.). This is the fastest way to get started.


Method 3 — Use gitignore.io (Now toptal.com/developers/gitignore)

This is a free website where you type your tools (like "Node", "Python", "macOS", "VSCode") and it generates a ready-to-use .gitignore file. Just copy and paste.


Method 4 — Use Git Itself


# Set a global gitignore file 
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global


.gitignore Syntax and Patterns

The rules inside a .gitignore file are called patterns. They are very flexible. Here is a full breakdown of how patterns work.

Pattern

What It Does

Example

# comment

Lines starting with # are comments

# This is a note


filename.ext

Ignores a specific file anywhere

secret.txt


foldername/

Ignores an entire folder

node_modules/


*.ext

Ignores all files with this extension

*.log


!filename

Un-ignores a previously ignored file

!important.log


/filename

Ignores only in the root folder

/config.env


folder/**

Ignores everything inside folder

build/**


**/*.ext

Ignores files in any subfolder

**/*.DS_Store


file?.txt

? matches exactly one character

file1.txt, filea.txt


[abc].txt

Matches one of the characters in [ ]

a.txt, b.txt, c.txt


Understanding Wildcards

Wildcards are special characters that match multiple files at once.

Wildcard Quick Reference

* (asterisk) — matches anything EXCEPT a slash. Use for file names.

** (double asterisk) — matches anything INCLUDING slashes. Use for deep paths.

? (question mark) — matches exactly ONE character.

[ ] (brackets) — matches any ONE character listed inside.

Negation Rules — The ! Trick

You can ignore a folder but keep specific files inside it using the ! prefix (negation).

# Ignore all log files *.log # But keep this important one !important.log # Ignore the build folder build/ # But keep one specific file inside it !build/README.mdcode
